_comment: A2A cross-bridge handoff config. Copy to handoff.local.json in your BRIDGE_HOME ($HOME/.agent-bridge by default), edit, then `chmod 0600` it. This file is data-only JSON — it is parsed, never executed. It carries peer-pair HMAC secrets, so it MUST be mode 0600 and MUST stay git-ignored. See docs/a2a-cross-bridge.md.
_bridge_id_comment: REQUIRED. This bridge's own id. It is the authenticated sender identity: the delivery runner signs it + sends it as the X-AGB-Peer header, and a peer receiver looks it up in its inbound peer allowlist. A receiving peer's config must list a peer entry whose 'id' equals this value.
_transport_comment: OPTIONAL (#1595). Selects the private-network transport. OMIT this block (or set kind=tailscale) for the default Tailscale substrate — existing configs behave EXACTLY as before. Set kind=cloudflare-warp-mesh to bind to a Cloudflare One / WARP Mesh device IP instead: the receiver then proves the bind IP is on a real local interface AND that WARP is connected+enrolled (warp-cli) before binding — CIDR shape alone is NOT proof, and it fails closed on wildcard/loopback/unassigned-local/WARP-disconnected/WARP-unregistered/warp-cli-absent. WARP-Mesh peers/listen key on a raw Mesh 'address' (Tailscale identity keys node_id/tailscale_name are rejected for this transport). WARP and Tailscale cannot run at once — pick ONE overlay transport per node, consistent across the mesh. This is Mesh/WARP-to-WARP private-IP connectivity, NOT the Cloudflare Tunnel/Access hostname model. Set kind=trusted-routed (#1758) for a private IP on a TRUSTED, router-protected corporate network with NO overlay client (WARP purged, Tailscale down): the bind proof is interface-assignment ONLY (the enrollment half is dropped — an explicit operator trust decision that the routed network itself is the boundary), while loopback/wildcard are STILL refused, an unknown kind STILL hard-fails, and HMAC + inbound_allowlist + the source-addr check are unchanged. A trusted-routed peer/listen keys on its raw routed-private 'address' (Tailscale identity keys rejected). A trusted-routed node CAN interoperate with warp-mesh peers (the sender selects the egress source per destination). See docs/a2a-cross-bridge.md (Transports). Examples: "transport": { "kind": "cloudflare-warp-mesh" } / { "kind": "trusted-routed" }.

listen._comment: The receiver binds to this tailnet IP ONLY. Leave 'address' empty to auto-select the first `tailscale ip`. The daemon FAILS CLOSED if the address is a wildcard (0.0.0.0/::), loopback, or cannot be proven to be in this node's actual Tailscale address set (the exact output of `tailscale ip`) — there is no CIDR-shape fallback, and if the tailscale CLI is unavailable the daemon refuses to serve.
listen._identity_comment: OPTIONAL (P0 self-heal): instead of (or in addition to) a raw 'address', you may key this node on a Tailscale IDENTITY that is resolved to the current IP at bind time via `tailscale status --json` — so an IP change after a tailnet re-login is picked up transparently. Precedence: 'node_id' (Tailscale StableID) wins over 'tailscale_name' (MagicDNS hostname or short HostName) wins over the legacy 'address'. When an identity is set, the resolved candidate STILL must pass the unchanged fail-closed bind proof (it must be in this node's `tailscale ip` set) — resolution only produces the candidate, it never weakens the proof. Examples (uncomment one): "node_id": "nDEADBEEF", "tailscale_name": "my-host" (or "my-host.your-tailnet.ts.net").
listen._identity_update_comment: OPTIONAL (P-self-heal-3, design §9.6): the receiver path for the signed `peer-identity-update` control message (default `/peer-identity-update`). A peer whose Tailscale IP changes pushes a signed update here (`agb a2a announce-identity`) so this node auto-updates THAT peer's stored identity — with no manual edit+restart. The receiver re-corroborates every claim against its OWN `tailscale status --json` and NEVER trusts the wire-asserted IP; it only updates an already-paired peer (not a discovery channel). You normally do not set this — the default is correct.
listen._hostname_comment: OPTIONAL (#16247): key this node on a fixed DNS 'hostname' (e.g. a Cloudflare-managed name that resolves to this node's current IP). Transport-AGNOSTIC (works on tailscale / cloudflare-warp-mesh / trusted-routed). Resolved live via getaddrinfo at bind/use-time — fail-closed (an unresolvable/empty/malformed name is a HARD error, never a fallback to 'address'), bounded (hard lookup timeout), and cached ~45s (a transient DNS blip inside the window is absorbed; once expired+unresolvable it fails closed). Full resolver precedence: 'node_id' > 'tailscale_name' > 'hostname' > legacy 'address'. The raw 'address' is IGNORED while 'hostname' is set (keep it for a tag-both/auto-switch rollout). Example: "hostname": "bridge-a.cosmaxcf.com".

_delivery_backoff_ceiling_comment: OPTIONAL (#1575 Part B). Ceiling in seconds for the exponential outbox-retry backoff (base 15s + full jitter). Default 120. Caps how long a recovered peer's high-attempt retry rows idle before the next deliver tick re-sends them; the 2026-06-06 tailnet incident showed the old 3600s ceiling left messages dormant for 16-60 min after the path recovered. Floor is 15s. Env override: BRIDGE_A2A_BACKOFF_CEILING_SECONDS.
_delivery_trust_peer_retry_after_comment: OPTIONAL (#1575 Part B). When false (default) a peer's Retry-After header is clamped to delivery_backoff_ceiling_seconds — the header is NOT HMAC-authenticated, so a transient/spoofed 503 cannot re-impose a multi-minute backoff. Set true ONLY if you intentionally trust a peer to dictate a longer backoff (e.g. a deliberately long maintenance window).
_transient_retention_comment: OPTIONAL (#1732). Default (in seconds) retention TTL for a peer marked class=transient: its outbox rows that hit delivery_max_attempts PARK (no terminal drop) until they have lived this long, then expire to dead(expired-transient-retention) so GC reclaims them. Default 604800 (7 days) so an overnight/weekend-asleep laptop still receives its messages when it wakes. Floored at 3600s. Per-peer override: caps.transient_retention_seconds.

peers._comment: Each peer is an ordered-pair relationship. 'address' is the peer's tailnet IP — the receiver enforces remote_addr == this value. 'secret' is the shared HMAC key for this pair; 'secret_next' is optional and only set during a rotation overlap window. 'inbound_allowlist' lists the LOCAL agents this peer may enqueue to (exact match, no wildcard).
peers._class_comment: OPTIONAL (#1732). 'class' marks how transient this peer is: 'persistent' (DEFAULT — server-class: a retryable failure dead-letters at delivery_max_attempts and the daemon raises a high-priority [A2A] outbox stuck admin alarm) or 'transient' (a personal laptop expected to sleep/disconnect: retryable failures NEVER terminally drop at max-attempts — they park and resume seamlessly when the peer returns via the peer-reachability UP transition, and the outbox-stuck alarm is suppressed). Permanent failures (missing secret/address/body, auth/HMAC, 4xx) STILL dead-letter immediately for ANY class. Opt in per-peer; existing peers default persistent (no behavior change). 'alarm_on_unreachable' (bool) overrides the class default for the alarm only. 'caps.transient_retention_seconds' / 'caps.stuck_alert_secs' override the per-peer TTL / alarm grace window. Example: "class": "transient", "alarm_on_unreachable": false.
peers._identity_comment: OPTIONAL (P0 self-heal): a peer may carry a Tailscale IDENTITY resolved to the peer's current IP at send-time via `tailscale status --json`, killing the stale-IP class (an IP change after the peer re-logs-in is picked up on the next send — no manual handoff.local.json edit). Precedence: 'node_id' (the peer's Tailscale StableID) wins over 'tailscale_name' (its MagicDNS hostname / short HostName) wins over the legacy 'address'. If an identity is set but cannot be resolved, the sender HARD-ERRORS (delivery retries; it never silently sends to a possibly-stale 'address'). Examples (uncomment one): "node_id": "nCAFED00D", "tailscale_name": "bridge-b-host" (or "bridge-b-host.your-tailnet.ts.net"). To convert an existing raw-`address` config to identity keying, run `agb a2a migrate-identity` (dry-run by default; --apply to write; keeps `address` as a fallback).
peers._peer_hostname_comment: OPTIONAL (#16247): key this PEER on a fixed DNS 'hostname' that resolves (split-horizon ok) to the peer's current IP. Transport-agnostic; live getaddrinfo at send-time AND in the receiver's source-address check (a multi-homed peer is accepted on ANY of its current A/AAAA records). Fail-closed/no-stale-fallback, bounded lookup, ~45s cache. Precedence 'node_id' > 'tailscale_name' > 'hostname' > 'address'; raw 'address' ignored while 'hostname' set. Example: "hostname": "bridge-b.cosmaxcf.com".
peers._peer_transport_comment: OPTIONAL (#1758). A per-peer 'transport' override declaring THIS peer is reachable over a DIFFERENT substrate than the node-level transport.kind — e.g. a cloudflare-warp-mesh node that ALSO talks to a corporate server over its routed LAN: mark that one peer "transport": { "kind": "trusted-routed" }. The override is SENDER-SIDE EGRESS-SOURCE selection ONLY: a warp-mesh destination egresses from this node's own Mesh listen.address (pinned source), while a trusted-routed/tailscale-marked peer gets NO source pin so the OS routing table picks the reachable egress interface (pinning the Mesh source to a routed IP yields 'Network is unreachable'). An UNMARKED peer inherits the node kind — existing same-substrate meshes are byte-unaffected. An unknown/typo peer transport.kind HARD-FAILS (fail-closed), same as the node-level key. It does NOT change the peer's 'address', the bind proof, HMAC, allowlist, or the source-address check. Example: "transport": { "kind": "trusted-routed" }.
